Pattern-driven RabbitMQ topology builder for Node.js microservices.
Pattern-driven RabbitMQ topology builder for Node.js microservices, built on Rascal.

    <Card icon="puzzle" title="Middleware Pipeline">
        Compose your RabbitMQ topology through a clean middleware pipeline. Each middleware receives
        the broker config, modifies it, and optionally hooks into the broker lifecycle.
    </Card>

        Fluent API with `withTopology().use(middleware).build()`. Define your base topology, layer on
        middleware, get back a configured Rascal broker.

        Written in TypeScript with full type safety. Strict mode, proper generics, no `any`-shaped
        escape hatches.

        Standing on the shoulders of a proven RabbitMQ library. Hoppity adds composable patterns on

        Request/reply with correlation IDs, configurable timeouts, and automatic reply queue
        management via `hoppity-rpc`.

What hoppity is and why it exists.

## What is hoppity?

Hoppity is a pattern-driven RabbitMQ topology builder for Node.js microservices, built on top of [Rascal](https://github.com/guidesmiths/rascal). It provides a composable middleware pipeline where each middleware receives a `BrokerConfig` and a shared `MiddlewareContext`, can modify both, and optionally return an `onBrokerCreated` callback for lifecycle hooks.

## The Problem

Rascal gives you a powerful, configuration-driven way to manage RabbitMQ topologies — exchanges, queues, bindings, publications, and subscriptions all declared in a single config object. But as your microservice estate grows, you end up with:

- Repeated boilerplate across services (the same exchange patterns, the same retry configurations)
- Topology config objects that balloon into hundreds of lines
- No clean way to share and compose common patterns (RPC, delayed publish, standard error handling)

## The Solution

Hoppity wraps Rascal's broker creation in a **middleware pipeline**. You start with a base topology and layer on reusable middleware that each handle a specific concern:

```typescript
import hoppity from "@apogeelabs/hoppity";
import { withRpc } from "@apogeelabs/hoppity-rpc";
import { withDelayedPublish } from "@apogeelabs/hoppity-delayed-publish";
import { withSubscriptions } from "@apogeelabs/hoppity-subscriptions";

const broker = await hoppity()
    .withTopology(baseTopology)
    .use(withRpc({ timeout: 5000 }))
    .use(withDelayedPublish({ delays: [1000, 5000, 30000] }))
    .use(withSubscriptions(handlers))
    .build();
```

Each middleware is a pure function that receives the current topology and a shared context, returns a (possibly modified) topology, and can optionally provide an `onBrokerCreated` callback for post-creation setup.

## Middleware Packages

| Package | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `@apogeelabs/hoppity` | Core pipeline + builder |
| `@apogeelabs/hoppity-rpc` | Request/reply with correlation IDs |
| `@apogeelabs/hoppity-delayed-publish` | TTL-based delayed message delivery |
| `@apogeelabs/hoppity-subscriptions` | Auto-wired subscription handlers |
| `@apogeelabs/hoppity-logger` | Custom logger injection |

## How the Pipeline Works

When you call `.build()`, three phases execute in order:

1. **Middleware execution** — Each middleware runs sequentially, receiving the current `BrokerConfig` and shared `MiddlewareContext`. Each returns a (possibly modified) topology and an optional `onBrokerCreated` callback.

2. **Broker creation** — The final topology is passed to `BrokerAsPromised.create()` via Rascal.

3. **Callback execution** — Each `onBrokerCreated` callback runs sequentially. If any callback fails, the broker is shut down before the error propagates.
Install hoppity and create your first middleware pipeline.

Main interface for the Rascal wrapper that provides the entry point to the middleware pipeline.

This wrapper provides two main entry points:
1. `withTopology()` - Start with an existing topology configuration
2. `use()` - Start with an empty topology and add middleware

MiddlewareFunction = (topology, context) => MiddlewareResult

Defined in: types.ts:178

Middleware function that can modify the topology and optionally provide a callback. Middleware functions are executed in the order they are added to the pipeline. Each middleware receives the current topology and a context object for sharing state.

Parameters

Section titled “Parameters”

topology

Section titled “topology”

BrokerConfig

The current topology configuration

context

Section titled “context”

MiddlewareContext

Context object for sharing state between middleware

Returns

Section titled “Returns”

MiddlewareResult

  • The modified topology and optional callback

Example

Section titled “Example”
// First middleware: sets up exchanges and shares info via context
const exchangeSetupMiddleware: MiddlewareFunction = (topology, context) => {
  // Modify topology to add exchanges
  const modifiedTopology = { ...topology };
  // ... add exchanges ...

  // Share exchange names with downstream middleware
  context.data.exchangeNames = ['user-events', 'order-events'];
  context.data.serviceName = 'user-service';

  return { topology: modifiedTopology };
};

// Second middleware: uses context from previous middleware
const queueSetupMiddleware: MiddlewareFunction = (topology, context) => {
  // Access data from previous middleware
  const exchangeNames = context.data.exchangeNames || [];
  const serviceName = context.data.serviceName;

  // Check if required middleware has run
  if (!context.middlewareNames.includes('exchangeSetupMiddleware')) {
    throw new Error('exchangeSetupMiddleware must run before queueSetupMiddleware');
  }

  // Use the shared data to set up queues
  const modifiedTopology = { ...topology };
  // ... set up queues bound to the exchanges ...

  return { topology: modifiedTopology };
};

Implementation of the Rascal wrapper that provides the main API for the middleware pipeline.

This object serves as the primary entry point for using the enhanced Rascal functionality.
It provides a fluent API for building complex broker configurations with middleware.
